name: Onboarding First Question Preview
overview: A delayed-signup onboarding pattern where the user is dropped directly into a tiny, delightfully simple first interaction with the core product (e.g. one sample exam question, one tap-to-translate sentence, one drag-drop) before any account-creation friction. Popularized by Canva's 'put a hat on a monkey' Beginner's Challenge and Duolingo's 'first lesson before signup' flow. By 2025 this has become the new default for consumer-grade learning and creative apps, formalized as 'gradual engagement' or 'aha-first onboarding' and combined with a fully skippable, 3-5 screen progressive-disclosure tour.
originator_or_company: Canva (Melanie Perkins, CEO/co-founder; 'Five Starter Challenges' incl. monkey-hat challenge, ca. 2013-2016). Duolingo (Luis von Ahn, CEO; Severin Hacker, CTO; Gina Gotthilf, former VP of Growth, ran the delayed-signup A/B test that lifted DAU ~20%). Pattern formalization in industry writing by Samuel Hulick (UserOnboarding.com), Andrew Chen (gradual engagement), Lenny Rachitsky (Lenny's Newsletter), and Appcues / Chameleon / Userpilot growth blogs.
key_resources: [The secret power of onboarding, w/ Canva's Melanie Perkins — Masters of Scale podcast](https://mastersofscale.com/melanie-perkins/)
[How Duolingo A/B tested delayed sign-ups to drive growth — Taplytics](https://taplytics.com/blog/duolingo-ab-test-onboarding/)
[Time to Value: The Key to Driving User Retention — Amplitude](https://amplitude.com/blog/time-to-value-drives-user-retention)
[How Emotion Helped Grow Our Startup to 2.8M Users in 20 Months — OnStartups (Canva)](https://www.onstartups.com/canva-user-journey)
core_mechanics: (1) ENTRY: Cold-start splash is replaced by a single, opinionated first-action card. No multi-screen value-prop carousel before the action. (2) THE TINY ACTION: One concrete, low-cognitive-load interaction that takes 5-30 seconds and produces a visible 'win'. Canva: drag a hat onto a cartoon monkey -> immediate visible result. Duolingo: translate a single short sentence by tapping word tiles -> instant correct/incorrect feedback. shikaq variant: answer ONE genuine ITパスポート / SPI / Boki / FE question with explanation. (3) DELIGHTFUL FEEDBACK: Spring animation, light haptic (selection / success), and a one-line congratulatory micro-copy ('Nice — you just answered your first ITパスポート question'). (4) PROGRESS PRIMER: A 3-5 step progress bar or dots is shown, but the action is the WHOLE first step — not 'page 1 of 5 explanatory screens'. (5) SKIPPABLE: A persistent 'Skip' link in the top-right (or top-left in JP) is always visible. Vevo's data shows adding skip lifted logins ~10% and signups ~6%. The skip is less prominent than the primary CTA but never hidden. (6) DELAYED SIGNUP: Account creation is deferred until AFTER the user has tasted value. Duolingo moved signup back several steps and saw ~20% DAU lift. Email/Apple Sign-In appears at a logical breakpoint (after the first lesson / after Q3 of a 5-question diagnostic). (7) PERSONALIZATION HOOK: Lightweight 1-2 question segmentation (Canva: 'I'm a [Marketer / Teacher / Student]'; shikaq: 'I'm preparing for [IP / FE / SPI / Boki]') that feeds the recommendation engine without ever blocking the action. (8) TIMING & MOTION: Apple HIG-aligned spring (damping ~20, stiffness ~300). Reduced Motion swaps to fade. Hairline borders, BlurView/Liquid Glass surfaces, no decorative drop shadows. (9) RECOVERY: Skipped onboarding can be re-entered from Settings -> 'Replay tour' (HIG: people should be able to revisit onboarding).
designer_or_researcher_voice: Melanie Perkins (Canva CEO, on Masters of Scale): the team designed onboarding to bring users 'from a state of fear to a state of play' — the monkey-hat challenge convinced people Canva was 'easy to learn and easy enough to explore' rather than a daunting professional design tool.

Gina Gotthilf (former Duolingo VP of Growth, paraphrased from Taplytics case study): moving the signup screen back a few steps in the funnel, after users had completed a short translation exercise, produced roughly a 20% lift in daily active users — the bet was that 'people will commit once they've felt the product work, not before.'

Apple Human Interface Guidelines (Onboarding): 'Help people enjoy your app or game right away by minimizing the learning required to get started... avoid showing splash screens, menus, agreements, or disclaimers when a new player opens your app.'

Samuel Hulick (UserOnboarding.com, paraphrased): the goal of the first run is not to teach the product, it is to engineer a moment where the user does the thing the product is for, and feels good about it — every screen between the user and that moment is a tax.

Jorge Mazal (former Duolingo CPO, Lenny's Newsletter, 2023): Duolingo's growth philosophy is to 'protect the channel' and remove every drop of avoidable onboarding friction; the lesson-before-signup flow is 'the single most important reason new users come back on day 2.'
metrics_or_evidence: Duolingo: delayed signup A/B test drove ~20% DAU increase (Taplytics, Gina Gotthilf). Vevo: adding a Skip option lifted logins ~10% and successful signups ~6%. Canva: personalized segment-driven onboarding produced ~10% activation lift (Appcues / Canva growth team). Slack: aha-first onboarding to 'send first message' contributes to industry-leading 93% DAU/WAU loyalty. Amplitude 2025 Product Benchmark Report (n=2,600+): products that deliver value within Day 1 see dramatically higher Day-7 and Day-14 retention; cutting time-to-value by 20% lifted ARR growth ~18% in mid-market SaaS (Amplitude 2024). Userpilot 2024 TTV benchmark (547 SaaS): median time-to-first-value ~1d 12h; industry leaders target <5 minutes. Onboarding flows with 3-5 screens and skip option see up to 5x engagement and 80%+ completion rates (UXCam, GenDesigns 2026). DoorDash / Airbnb / Wise all use guest-mode equivalents of this pattern as a primary funnel optimization.
criticisms: (1) Novelty Effect: the 'delight' of dragging a hat onto a monkey or answering one cute question is a one-time hit; metrics can look great in week 1 but plateau. Critics (Nielsen Norman Group, NN/g 2024) warn that early-cycle activation lift does not always translate to Day-30 retention without a deeper habit loop behind it. (2) Misleading Difficulty Curve: a deliberately easy first interaction can mis-set expectations — exam-prep users may feel betrayed when real ITパスポート / FE questions are far harder than the preview, producing churn at lesson 2-3 (related to 'expectation violation' in UX research). (3) Privacy / data-collection critique: deferred signup is sometimes used as a wedge to harvest behavioral data anonymously before the user has consented; this is on the EU EDPB radar (2024-2025 dark-pattern guidelines). (4) Overjustification risk: framing learning as a tiny game can crowd out intrinsic motivation for serious adult learners (Deci & Ryan, classic SDT critique applied to gamified onboarding by Andrzej Marczewski, HEXAD). (5) Means-Ends Reversal: users who optimise for the cheerful tutorial dopamine may bounce off the slower, harder real material — a known issue in Duolingo's own qualitative research.
failure_cases: (1) Apps that gate the very first action behind email + password + push permission + tracking permission see well-documented 60-80% drop-off in the install -> first-action funnel (Apphud, AppAgent 2025). (2) Tutorials that demand a multi-step coachmark tour BEFORE the first action repeatedly underperform; Vevo, Slack, and Duolingo all moved away from these. (3) Forced-signup walls (no skip, no guest mode) have triggered FTC enforcement statements (Oct 2021) on dark patterns and are increasingly cited under EU DSA Article 25 (2024-2025). (4) Pre-2019 Duolingo experimented with a more 'serious EdTech' signup-first flow; engagement stagnated until they reverted to the lesson-before-signup pattern. (5) Several Japanese exam apps (e.g. パターン化された ITパスポート 過去問 アプリ) show heavy ad-walled or login-walled first screens; their App Store reviews repeatedly flag the friction, and the free '5問チャレンジ' style apps consistently outperform them on conversion and reviews.
antipatterns: (1) Fake first action (a tutorial that PRETENDS to be the product but is a scripted animation) — destroys trust the moment the user hits the real product. Use the real component, just with a curated easy input. (2) 'Skip' button that's invisible / only on the last page / triggers a guilt modal ('Are you sure? You'll miss out!') — a textbook FTC-flagged dark pattern and HIG violation. (3) Forcing notification / tracking / location permissions before the first action — Apple App Store Review reject risk plus measurable funnel collapse. (4) 10+ slide value-prop carousels before a single tap; modern HIG explicitly discourages 'splash screens, menus, agreements, or disclaimers' as openers. (5) Onboarding that never re-surfaces — users who skipped need a discoverable Settings -> 'Replay intro' affordance per HIG. (6) Difficulty-mismatched preview (e.g. showing a kindergarten-level math question for a CPA-level exam app) — produces predictable churn at lesson 2. (7) Using the preview to harvest data anonymously and then surprise-binding it to an account at signup without explicit notice — emerging EU GDPR / EDPB violation risk.
applicability_to_shikaq: Strong direct fit. The shikaq persona (20s Japanese women, career-switching, iPad-in-cafe, intimidated by thick reference books) is exactly the 'state of fear' Perkins designed the monkey hat to defuse — only the medium is an exam question, not a cartoon. Apple HIG translation: (a) FIRST RUN: open straight into a single, beautifully typeset ITパスポート sample question on a Liquid Glass card. SF Pro Display Title 2 for question number, Body 17pt for the stem, four hairline-bordered choice rows. No navigation chrome. (b) THE TAP: selecting a choice triggers a spring scale + light haptic (selection); correct -> systemGreen check + notification.success haptic, incorrect -> systemRed cross + notification.error haptic. (c) RESULT CARD: shows answer + 1-paragraph explanation in Footnote (13pt) + 'You just experienced shikaq. Want 4 more like this?' CTA. (d) DIAGNOSTIC LOOP: the next 4 questions are calibrated to estimate weak/strong areas; segmentation question ('Which exam are you targeting? IP / FE / SPI / Boki / Not sure yet') appears AFTER Q1, never before. (e) DEFERRED SIGNUP: signup with Apple/Google/Email appears only after Q5, framed as 'Save your diagnostic + 7-day free trial' — Apple Sign-In as primary CTA per HIG. (f) SKIP: persistent text-button 'スキップ' top-right, returns user to the main Today view in a guest mode that gradually layers the signup ask at high-value moments (after a lesson completion, after a streak milestone). (g) HEXAD coverage: Achiever (immediate score), Free Spirit (skip available always), Player (calibrated diagnostic), Philanthropist (later, study-group invites). Avoid Disruptor / Player-only mechanics. (h) Critically: keep the preview question genuinely representative. No artificially-easy 'kindergarten ITパスポート' question — pick a medium-easy genuine 過去問 to set honest expectations.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). This is the single highest-leverage onboarding mechanic and must ship in v1 — the entire activation funnel for a B2C subscription learning app pivots on it. Building it later means rebuilding routing, auth deferral, and the diagnostic engine from scratch, which is materially more expensive than getting it right at launch.
implementation_size: M. Requires: (1) a question-rendering component reused from the main Practice screen (so the preview is the real product), (2) auth-deferred routing (guest session in Supabase Auth or a local-only state until signup), (3) diagnostic scoring lightweight model (rules-based is fine for v1), (4) a 'replay intro' entry in Settings, (5) skip-state persistence. No new backend tables required if questions table is already seeded. Largest hidden cost is the auth-deferral plumbing (RLS policies that allow read-only access to public questions for unauthenticated sessions, plus secure carry-over of pre-signup answer history into the new account).
integration_points: Components: new `OnboardingFirstQuestion.tsx` that wraps the existing question component from `screens/Practice`, `OnboardingDiagnosticResult.tsx`. App.tsx: pre-Auth gate that routes first-run users to the onboarding flow before AuthProvider's gated content. AuthProvider: extend to support guest session with a local UUID until real signup; on signup, merge guest `quiz_results` into the new `auth.uid()`. DB tables: `questions` (existing, needs a `is_onboarding_sample` boolean or a tag for curated previews), `quiz_results` (existing, allow nullable user_id or guest_id column with RLS bypass for anonymous inserts), `profiles` (existing, set `onboarding_completed_at`). Routes: new `/onboarding/preview` and `/onboarding/result`. Sidebar: hidden during onboarding. Settings screen: add 'チュートリアルをもう一度見る' row that re-enters the flow. Sentry: tag onboarding events so funnel drop-off is observable.
risks: (1) Auth-deferral plumbing complexity — Supabase RLS for genuinely-anonymous reads + later attribution of guest quiz_results to a new auth.uid() is non-trivial and a known footgun (race conditions on signup). (2) Difficulty calibration for the preview question: too easy and we set the wrong expectations (churn at lesson 2); too hard and we re-create the 'thick textbook fear' the persona came to escape. Needs human curation per certification. (3) Apple App Store Review may push back if the deferred signup hides the subscription terms — must surface ¥980/month + 7-day trial language at the signup wall, not after. (4) Japanese localization risk: 'スキップ' label and modal copy must feel natively Apple-like, not translated marketing copy; Hiragino Sans + sentence-final particles need native review. (5) Scope creep: the onboarding flow is the most-touched product surface for marketing — pressure to add 'tell us about yourself' segmentation screens will be constant; resist anything that delays the first tap. (6) Privacy: anonymous answer data collected pre-signup must be disclosed in the privacy policy and APP プライバシー labels; otherwise EU/JP regulatory exposure.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. The pattern aligns directly with Apple HIG Onboarding ('help people enjoy your app right away'), Launching ('avoid splash screens, menus, agreements, or disclaimers'), and the Less-but-better / Deference / Clarity foundations. Spring motion, Reduced Motion fallback, and SF Symbols / Material Symbols for the choice icons fit the existing shikaq design system without modification. The only adaptation needed is to ensure the 'delightful' feedback stays restrained: a single spring scale + a hairline systemGreen check, NOT a confetti burst or a Duolingo-style mascot reaction — the latter would conflict with Apple's Deference principle. Liquid Glass surfaces for the question card on iPad keep the layering hierarchy correct.
ethical_score: positive. Compared to dark-pattern alternatives (forced signup walls, hidden skip, surprise paywalls, escalating notifications), the First Question Preview is one of the most user-respectful onboarding patterns: it puts value before commitment, makes skip a first-class citizen, and gives the user accurate information about what they're signing up for. Center for Humane Technology's 'time well spent' criteria favor this pattern provided two conditions are met: (a) the preview question is honestly representative of real difficulty, not a manipulative low-bar trick, and (b) skipped users can complete the flow without coercion or guilt copy. No measurable burnout / dependency / time-regret risk in the literature.
evidence_quality: company_case_study. Strong evidence base in published growth-team write-ups (Duolingo via Taplytics & Lenny's Newsletter, Canva via Appcues / Masters of Scale, Slack & Dropbox via Amplitude benchmarks) and in Amplitude's 2025 Product Benchmark Report (n=2,600+) plus Userpilot's 2024 TTV benchmark (n=547). Not RCT-level rigour, but consistent directional findings across many independent companies and a HIG-codified recommendation in 2025. Some specific lift numbers (Duolingo +20% DAU, Vevo +10% logins) are second-hand and should be treated as directional rather than causal.
localization_fit_japan: high. The 20s Japanese career-switcher persona is uniquely friction-averse on signup compared to US peers — Japanese mobile users typically abandon at email/SMS verification far earlier (industry surveys repeatedly show JP onboarding completion ~10-15pt below US). A guest-mode lesson-before-signup pattern, executed in clean Apple-native Japanese typography (Hiragino Sans), maps onto the persona's '黙って見せて' (just show me, don't pitch me) preference. Japanese exam-prep market already has free '5問チャレンジ' style apps (e.g. Impress's チャレンジ！ITパスポート), so the pattern is culturally pre-validated. Localization caveats: copy must avoid英語直訳 marketing tone ('Let's go!' / 'Awesome!'); use restrained, polite forms ('解いてみる' / '次の問題へ'). Skip label '後で' or 'スキップ' both acceptable. Respect Japanese reluctance to share personal info pre-signup by ONLY collecting target-exam segmentation, not name/age/job during the preview.
